title: Imaging GHz surface acoustic wave modes in electrostricted LaAlO$_3$/SrTiO$_3$ heterostructures
authors: Ranjani Ramachandran, Sayanwita Biswas, Prithwijit Mandal, Kyoungjun Lee, Madeleine Msall, Chang-Beom Eom, Patrick Irvin, Jeremy Levy, Mingyun Yuan
abstract: The LaAlO$_3$/SrTiO$_3$ (LAO/STO) interface hosts a gate-tunable superconducting two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) which can be programmed to create quantum devices such as ballistic electron waveguides and quantum dots. To fully exploit this platform for quantum transport, a key requirement is the ability to shuttle single electrons, electron pairs, and other exotic states between spatially separated devices with precision. Surface acoustic waves (SAWs), which travel along the surface of a solid, offer a powerful route to achieve this through their moving electrical potential that captures and transfers electrons. %acoustoelectric coupling. In particular, SAWs in the GHz regime enable fast, controlled transport of individual quantum particles. Although this approach is well-explored in GaAs-based 2DEG, SAW generation in STO remains largely unexplored due to the lack of intrinsic piezoelectricity at room temperature. Here, we investigate room-temperature SAWs in LAO/STO and observe SAW modes up to 2.2 GHz with very low propagation loss of the order $10^{-3}$ dB per wavelength. To directly visualize these modes, we employ Atomic Acoustic Force Microscopy (AAFM), achieving sub-micron resolution imaging of the SAW wave forms, providing insight into the electrostriction-induced SAW generation mechanism. Our measurements indicate a shear horizontal-type mode, which provides the ability to couple to in-plane degrees of freedom for future acoustoelectric and quantum device applications. This work studies the fundamentals of SAW excitation and propagation on STO, a widely used and commercially available substrate, enabling straightforward coupling of SAWs to a broad range of materials that can be grown or transferred onto STO.
summary: This paper demonstrates how to generate high-frequency surface acoustic waves in LAO/STO heterostructures using electric-field-induced electrostriction. By identifying new acoustic modes and achieving extremely low signal loss, the work provides a new method for controlling electrons and other quantum states in oxide-based platforms.
main_problem: The challenge of generating and controlling Surface Acoustic Waves (SAWs) in SrTiO3-based heterostructures due to the lack of intrinsic piezoelectricity at room temperature.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated GHz-frequency SAW generation via electrostriction in LAO/STO, identifying two distinct modes including a previously unobserved shear horizontal-type mode with ultra-low propagation loss.
method: The study uses RF spectroscopy (VNA) to measure S-parameters and Atomic Acoustic Force Microscopy (AAFM) for sub-micron resolution imaging of SAW waveforms.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of LaAlO3/SrTiO3 (LAO/STO) heterostructures where a DC bias induces electrostriction, creating a thin-film waveguide capable of supporting acoustic modes.
key_observables: S-parameters (transmission S21 and reflection S11), SAW propagation loss, SAW velocity, and spatial waveform imaging via AAFM.
important_parameters: GHz frequency range (up to 2.2 GHz), wavelengths of 2, 4, and 8 micrometers, and propagation loss on the order of 10^-3 dB per wavelength.
main_assumptions: The identification of the second mode as SH-type is noted as a possibility rather than a definitive classification.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the electrostriction mechanism, experimental setup, and S-parameter transmission peaks; Figure 2 shows the enhancement of transmission with DC bias.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for SAW-based electron shuttling, describes the electrostriction-induced SAW generation mechanism, presents RF and AAFM experimental results, and discusses the implications for future quantum device integration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Illustration of centrosymmetric STO in the absence of electric field. (b) Illustration of a tetragonal structural deformation when a DC electric field is applied on STO. (c) Schematic of the experimental setup used to measure the S-parameters. A bias-tee is connected between the IDTs and the VNA to apply the DC bias VDC1 and VDC2. (d) S parameters measured from delay line DL2 of kSAW ¼ 2 lm with VDC1 ¼ VDC2 ¼ 30 V. (e) Velocity of SAW modes as a function of normalized thickness (t=kSAW) under VDC1 ¼ VDC2 ¼ 30 V (solid symbol) against the calculated curve, vTh.

Figure 1(c) shows an illustration of a typical sample layout and the experimental setup. We started with four unit cells of LAO on STO (001) grown as described in the supplementary material, Sec. I and selectively etched away the LAO by reactive ion etching47 to define the “canvases” containing the 2DEG, shown by the white dot­ ted line. The IDTs were patterned using standard electron beam lithography and metalized with a stack of Ti/Al/Ti with thicknesses of 10/50/10 nm, leading to a metallization thickness of t ¼ 70 nm. DC biases, VDC1 and VDC2, are applied across IDT fingers of opposite polarities in the input and output IDTs, respectively, to induce elec­ trostriction on STO. We...

(b) S21max as a function of VDC2. (c) S21max decaying with time after zeroing VDC1 and VDC2 from 30 V. The initial response is fitted to an exponential decay S21max ¼ Ae−t=s with A ¼ −50:763 dBm and s ¼ 55:88 s. (d) Time-domain echoes of S21 from DL4c with 250 kSAW delay at VDC1 ¼ VDC2 ¼ 30 V. Peak values are fitted to a straight line to extract c listed in Table I.

SAW propagation is not hindered by the step. (d) Scan dSAW in the x-direction as a function of applied RF frequency, f. (e) Cross section of the two SAW modes indicated by arrows in (d). (f) Comparison between the S21 and peak value of dSAW averaged over x ¼ 20 lm for f stepping in (d). Arrows signal the opposite trends of the peak height.

Figure 3(d) shows AAFM measurements of the SAW signal along the delay line at different frequencies. Surprisingly, the SAW amplitudes are larger for the slower mode at 2.158 GHz than the faster mode at 2.17GHz. In contrast, S21 is consistently higher for the higher frequency mode, cf. Fig. 3(f).
